name: Truffle / Graal AST as IR
summary: In the GraalVM Truffle framework, the language implementer writes an AST interpreter — but the AST is also the IR. Partial evaluation specializes the AST against observed types and values, producing an effectively-compiled version of the same tree. Same data structure for source-faithful AST and optimization substrate.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: In the GraalVM Truffle framework, the language implementer writes an *AST interpreter* — but the AST is also the IR. Partial evaluation specializes the AST against observed types and values, producing an effectively-compiled version of the same tree. There is no separate IR phase; the AST nodes carry inline caches, profiling, and specialization metadata that make them executable both interpretively and (after Graal partial evaluation) as native code.

sections.title: Futamura projection made concrete
sections.content:
This is the Futamura projection made concrete: the partial evaluator (Graal) plus the interpreter (the AST + node specializations) is the compiler. Truffle's contribution is showing that the same data structure can be both the source-faithful AST and the optimization substrate, eliminating a translation layer.

The approach is industrial-strength rather than academic: language implementers write specialised AST nodes (with `@Specialization` and inline-cache machinery) and let Graal's partial evaluator generate competitive native code from the resulting tree.

sections.title: Production languages on Truffle
sections.content:
Production languages on Truffle as of 2026-04: **GraalJS** (V8-replacement for Java embedding), **GraalPy** (CPython-compatible Python with claimed competitive performance to PyPy on numeric workloads, Java-embedding-friendly), **TruffleRuby** (Ruby with C-extension support), **FastR** (R), **Espresso** (Java-on-Java for sandboxing), **Sulong** (LLVM bitcode interpreter for C/C++/Fortran).

The Truffle pattern proves that a *single partial-evaluator infrastructure* (Graal) hosting many language frontends produces competitive performance per language with much less per-language engineering than handwriting a JIT compiler. The cost is the GraalVM runtime dependency and Java-VM-shaped behaviour at the host boundary.
